feedback.text: For decades, computer software has been sold on physical media that worked without the need of any online verification. Even though updates/patches for the software are often no longer available, the base form that has been delivered to customers is still usable now, if you can find a suitable machine to run it on (or emulate an older one). This is true for application software as well as games. Companies now want to end this by only selling "digital temporary licenses", the idea being that even though you pay money for the item you are not able to use it anymore whenever the seller sees fit (without announcing this upfront). This goes against everything that customer protection laws stand for. If I purchase an item with my money, it should be mine to keep and use however and whenever I see fit. With regards to software, I can see that the upkeep for an only-online program could be too high after years of service. Yet, even multiplayer games like Ultima Online still have a thriving community after being abandoned by their creators - made possible by unofficial servers the original makers have no say in, but also no obligation towards in any way. For single player games and single user applications though, there is no reason at all why they should stop working at any given time. There could be technical limitations, and it is fine if a company doesn't want to update software to a newer version of an operating system. But the user who purchased the software should be able to use it on older hardware/operating systems as long as he wants after making a one-time purchase that is labeled as such. Current efforts by software companies to turn the narrative regarding online games towards multiplayer titles are bending the truth, because recent initiatives were about not artificially limiting the lifespan of these single user games and applications by tethering them to an online check which is not necessary for them to function in the first place.
